speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 2. Before the break, we were talking about the Jews being rounded up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were rounded up in Luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we, my mother and I and the 13 occupants of that ghetto dwelling-- women, as I said before, 10 women and three children, two children younger than I-- the lorry stopped in front of the place. We were loaded onto the place, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] strange people opposite me, sitting opposite. There would be the Jews, turning in the eastern direction as the truck went along, trying to make contact with God. And they were calling out, [NON ENGLISH]. God was on holiday, so He never heard them, because they shot 17,000 Jews on that spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: You might ask, how do you talk to me now? I was there. We were undressed like the rest with German perverted precision, precision. We had to put [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pants, our panties rather, and dresses, on different little heaps.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were mountains of clothing. And as soon as you undressed-- it might be a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trivial detail, but the Poles who were assisting the Germans, they were there in a shot, looking through the seams of the clothing in case you buried something, you had something in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they would turn around to the Jews who were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shot, saying what do you bastards need this for? You don't need anything any longer. That was the Polish comment. Where was this spot?

speaker: interviewee
text: This spot was called Lubianka, in the Lubianka Hills, outside Luck. Now I come to the miracle. Suddenly, in the shooting-- I call it the shooting gallery-- the volley of shots stopped for a second, and someone said, stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked up, and it was my biggest, my big brother's friend, a Polish doctor called Richard Ludwig. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he asked me, how come you're here? And I lied. They took us by mistake. You ask me how was Richard there-- Richard, Dr. Richard Ludwig.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German crew-- I only got close to one-- the shooting battalion, they were dead drunk. And I was-- one was so drunk that he lay, prostrate, on the ground. So Richard-- it must've been providence that he came just at that moment, when we were just holding, in our hand, the bit of our false papers, identifying us as Catholics, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he told the Germans he would pledge, he would vouch for these Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, while we were in the ghetto, she procured the false papers through some Polish bureaucrat, working in a German office. They had a stamp on. They had our names on. And they had-- gave the religions, the religion as Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Caroline Ryjkowska. I'll spell the surname for you, R-Y-J-K-O-W-S-K-A. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the test for Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the test applied by Germans to Poles suspected to be Jews was to make them, to make them pray and to chant their Polish prayers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I told you I grew up in a Catholic school, and I knew it inside out. My mother came from a religious home. She spoke very good Polish, but the doctor lightly kicked her and said, try and faint. And she gave an Oscar performance that she was fatigued [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the man, man who gathered himself from the ground said to us, [NON ENGLISH], get out-- get the hell out of here. We grabbed a couple of ill-fitting garments, my mother and I, and we left. However, before I tell you-- I continue with this, I want to tell you about the incidents. I just told you about the Poles who looked at Jewish clothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw these men, these bearded Jews, who were pushed to the pit. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Neat German precision, they had to put their clothing on the ground, but they never parted with their skull caps. When they fell, this was in my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They held onto their caps, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they chanted. I couldn't understand the chants. But it was a kind of an act of defiance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Neither did the German know what they were chanting. They were talking to God. That's what I wanted-- that's an unforgettable moment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I shall tell you, getting out of hell, we didn't know which direction to take. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we walked for about two days. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then we were hiding. We came across a stretch of a field [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was half harvested, and it had stalks of beans. And we were hiding in this-- behind those beans for a few days. But when we got up one morning, we could see the ground sort of getting balder. They were harvesting on the other side of the field. And they would probably get closer to us. Not talking about diarrhea or-- because we ate just the leaves from the beans. We had nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mother and I reached the main road. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And suddenly, a, a milk delivery truck stopped. And it was a friend of mine, slightly older, probably, than I and using his brother's driving license because he was too young to drive. And he was delivering milk to a German cooperative.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had a friend, a Polish, a kind Polish friend, [INAUDIBLE] guy, across the bridge. But we would never have got across that bridge ourselves. Perhaps we would, but there was a sentry on that bridge, and what do you know what the sentry is going to ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this friend brought us across the bridge. He was a kind, kind boy. He gave us a glass of milk before he turned around with his load.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked up this Polish family. Their name was Satowsky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the girl was a friend of mine. I was there for many years and always made very welcome. But at that time, they were horrified when they saw me, so they're pleased I was alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They could not help. They would not accept my offer that we knew they had relatives in the country and we were prepared to work for nothing. They wouldn't have a bar of it. What happens, this woman's husband-- this is what she gave as an excuse not to help us-- was an officer in the Polish army. And she got word that he was stationed near Frankfurt, but not Frankfurt on Main, but Frankfurt on the Oder. That was closer to Prussia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And six of these officers escaped because they were to be recruited from mining work. And she told me that the house would be under surveillance and I should get out. And that's what I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- our next step was my mother's laundry woman. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother's laundry woman, who was a Ukrainian, wanted to give her sanctuary. But you couldn't expect, the very few people who are prepared to do it, to give sanctuary to two people, to doubly endanger themselves. And my mother told me, unless we find a place for you, I am not going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Consequently one night, we decided we would walk during the curfew and Mother would wait for me at the house of another friend of mine. There's a long story with this friend, but I'll try and, and shorten it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This girl lived in Luck, but she went to school in Lwow. She was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] several years in a sanatorium for TB. And all the girls in our class were frightened. You know, there was a stigma attached to people like that. And we became-- she was wonderful girl. And we became great friends, and she took me in. And I signaled to my mother. And she went to her washer lady, who I knew where she lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed overnight at Christina's. I want to say Christina was nearly four years older than I, because-- having been in the sanatorium. And she was sent to Lwow to school because she had an aristocratic aunt. We were in, in Lwow. We were of-- we're as different as, as, as [INAUDIBLE] from one another. But we were attached. And I was actually-- I was her [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] medium for distributing love letters to her boyfriends and getting them back, so-- but there's a little childish, naive sort of connection out of it. She already had a friend who could have been her father, and he was a Volksdeutsche called Huber. When she introduced him to me and she said his name was Huber, I thought to myself, next thing she's going to tell me, he's German. And he was. He was a Volksdeutsche. But I have to tell you this as a prelude to what it all amounts to, to my survival. Huber was in the administration of Todt building. Todt, I don't know whether you are familiar-- the Todt Organization, named by former [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think armament minister for-- in Germany. He arranged-- it was a widespread organization which, in occupied territories, had-- was responsible for extending railway lines and building fortifications. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And Huber tutored me, how I go into the office. I don't look at him, and I don't know him. And he will give me a job in the kitchen. He knew who I was because Christina told him. Anyhow, I secured this job in the kitchen peeling potatoes. I was peeling potatoes till my cuticles sort of were bleeding. I was hoping they were bombs, and I was throwing them into water all the time. To make it short, my job-- also why I'm telling you, it might sound trivial-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my job also included, in the afternoon, cleaning the room of four German supervisors. You see, the Todt Organization brought with them workers, skilled workers from another place, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a hole. So they brought some skilled workers from Warthegau, electricians and people like that. But the Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON ENGLISH] as they were called, the supervisors, they kept sort of-- they separated themselves from them. But I was cleaning the room of these four Germans. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And in the evening, when the men came from work, I helped in the kitchen to dish out the, the soups for them and whatever dinner it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From that place, once I looked around and could see what I could steal for my mother, who was starving, I made a few trips to this washer woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forget her name just now. It escapes me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I gave my-- I stole from the Germans for my mother. And one day I came, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and this woman was outside. When I knocked-- it was a long walk for a little girl to go through a frozen field. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At home I was scared to go to the toilet at night. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, she came out and she said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go away, not out of malice, but meaning well. My Polish neighbors informed on your mother. The police took her away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She's not here, and they're waiting for you to denounce you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She said that she was brought to the kommandantur, why she-- why, why my mother was in her place. My mother, to protect her, must have told her that she had a key and she let herself in. So they kept punishing her, why she gave the Jewess the key. Anyhow, her son worked for the Germans. And she was an old lady, so they let her go. And she warned me, and I got out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I returned-- you see, I had no perception of curfew, not curfew. I had to see my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on my way back, I sort of lied down in snow and cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had no adult sort of advice what to do. When I got back to the Todt building-- because they provided dormitory for its workers-- a torch, someone shone a torch into my eyes. And I told you before about these German supervisors. It was one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beginning of winter, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was snow and rain. My face must have been flushed. And I was crying. And all he wanted to know, [NON-ENGLISH]. Who beat you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a child. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his name was Paul Moebius, M-O-E-B-I-U-S. He was always kind [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, I was there as a Pole, not as a Jew. I was-- I secured this job as a Polish girl, not a Jewish girl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I confessed to him, when he kept pestering me, how come you're here at this time? It's a curfew. You're not allowed to be out. I told him, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he looked at me. I could see some pity in his eyes, but I couldn't get into the man's mind. You know, all he said to me, go to bed, child.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I reached my cot, I thought to myself, I was stupid. What were you looking for? For a father figure? Where is your mother? Is she in heaven? In a grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told myself to myself, I remember those marksmen from the liquidation center that shot all these Jews. And I thought, that's-- I am next. So a voice inside me said, you must get out, and do it now. So I just got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Where do I go? The ghetto is closed. My mother isn't there. There was-- there were rumors-- and you grasped at straws-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that the owner and founder of a hospital in Luck had some connection. He was not a Pole. He was a Belarus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a white Russian, that is. That he had some connection with the partisans. It occurred to me that if you could get yourself into partisan unit, you might be useful to them, and they wouldn't kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So what do I do? I knock at this hospital door. An old nurse comes out, asks me what I'm in pain. One minute I say yes, and then I said no because I didn't know where I was sore.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, she took me in and told me-- put me on a bench and asked me to wait for the doctor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the doctor came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said to me-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I whispered. It was very close. You know, the staff was close. And even though I didn't have much adult experience, I wasn't stupid enough to think that if he had such a controversial side occupation that he would share it with the staff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, all of this, I told him that his daughter was a friend, his daughter [NON-ENGLISH]-- his name was-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- that she was a friend of my brother's. You looked for any connection. She really was a friend of my brother's. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You looked for any connection that could, you know, could be a, a linchpin in some relationships, in some contact. And all of a sudden, he said to me, put your finger in your mouth and throw up. I wondered why he wanted to make me sick, you know, but then it dawned on me. He said there are too many spies and too many eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can get you to Rovno. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't spell out partisan unit, but I knew. He said but they-- that would take 10 days to a week. He wanted that I pretended I had appendicitis. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And before I could turn around, the nurses came and jabbed needles in to me. And I was operated on. They took out my appendix, which I didn't know where it was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the third day after that operation, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his younger daughter came and threw my clothing onto the bed, took it out from the closet, and said, the police, they arrested my father. They must not find you here. Get out. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Where do I go? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I was leaving, she said to me, [SOBBING] God be with you. But God was on holiday. He never looked in my direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I could hear shots within the hospital grounds. It wasn't a huge hospital. And I knew they were after someone more important than I. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I waited till it became more serene, and then I went out. I thought I wanted to avoid the main road. And as I came out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was suddenly flattened to the ground by a huge Alsatian. It sounds like a story from the, from a fairy tale. And when I looked up, I couldn't believe it. There was a uniformed German policeman standing over me and grinning. It was a roundup of Polish girls, recruited for work to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He called the dog Rolf, and at that moment, I wasn't sure whether I was more afraid of the beast or the man. Anyhow, I looked around, and there was a group of Polish girls, a couple younger than myself. The others were 15, 16, 17. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were on the lookout for the next victim. And I joined the girls. The scenery's changed, and your performance, your acting performance, had to change. You were an actress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the girls cried because they had to leave their families. So I cried because not to invite suspicion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They brought us to a very crowded railway, Luck railway station. As we walked through the streets, all the Gentiles that saw us kept sort of crossing themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They saw young girls, you know, even though Polish girls. But I thought I'd defend my position because I thought that prospective Polish Christian workers were more worth to, to the Nazis alive than dead. So I did as they did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a long time-- and one boy tried to escape, and they shot him. They kept the girls separate from the boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The train came. First it was a [NON-ENGLISH] train. But it didn't-- it just went at a snail pace a very short distance, and they shunted us onto another rail line, set of rails.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of tape 2.
